behaviors.description: A static prompt-template lint check rejects phase-specific instruction leakage copied from SKILL.md.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is pure pattern-detection logic on a string — does the lint function correctly identify forbidden text. No module handoff is needed; a function takes template text and returns pass/fail. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or subprocess involved — the lint check operates on in-memory strings. LLM-DEP: No model call. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would be required only if the lint check had to dynamically load and diff against SKILL.md in a pipeline; a well-designed lint check receives the candidate template as input.
behaviors.test_description: Call the lint function with (a) a template containing a verbatim SKILL.md phase instruction and assert it raises or returns a rejection, and (b) a clean template that omits those instructions and assert it passes. Both inputs are in-memory strings — no filesystem or network required.

behaviors.description: The fully assembled prompt produced by the real test harness (after interpolation/fixtures) still contains no phase-instruction leakage.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure this behavior guards against is that the interpolation/fixture pipeline itself injects leakage even when individual templates are clean. That failure only surfaces when the harness modules are wired together. A unit test of the lint check alone cannot catch this. REAL-INFRA: The harness reads fixture files from the local filesystem, but no external network or subprocess is required — an in-process run with real local files is sufficient. LLM-DEP: We only inspect the assembled prompt text, not any model response. REJECTION-GATE: System would require real HTTP or subprocess; the failure is in module wiring, not OS behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full test-harness prompt-assembly pipeline (fixture loading → interpolation → template rendering) against real skill/fixture files on the local filesystem. Capture the final assembled prompt string and assert that none of the phase-specific instruction patterns from SKILL.md appear in it. No model call is made.

behaviors.description: The test invocation loads skill context from the plugin/skill files and validates behavior from that context rather than from hard-coded prompt rules.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Confirming that the prompt includes loaded skill context (structural) is Integration. But 'validates behavior from that context' means the test must actually demonstrate that the model produces skill-driven outputs — not just that the skill text appears in the prompt. A mocked model always returns whatever stub is configured; it cannot show that behavior is context-driven. LLM-DEP: Correctness depends on whether the real LLM responds differently to prompts with vs. without skill context — that is inherently non-deterministic and model-dependent. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would suffice only if 'validates behavior' meant checking prompt structure; the phrasing explicitly means behavioral validation, requiring a real model call.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the test invocation twice against a real LLM: once with skill context loaded from the plugin files, once with skill context stripped. Assert that (a) the run with skill context produces outputs consistent with the skill's expected behaviors, and (b) the run without skill context produces noticeably different outputs — confirming behavior is context-driven, not hard-coded.

behaviors.description: If the relevant SKILL.md section is removed or degraded, the behavioral test fails with the same user-style prompt.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: The entire point of this behavior is that a real LLM's output changes when skill content is degraded. With a mocked or stubbed model, the stub returns the same answer regardless of what is in SKILL.md, so the test would never fail on degradation — the failure mode is invisible without a real model. MOCK-HIDE: Any mocking of the model makes this test trivially pass, permanently hiding the regression signal. REJECTION-GATE: System is not required because no external subprocess or real HTTP (beyond the model API) is involved. The step count is one inference call, so Workflow is not needed.
behaviors.test_description: Run the behavioral smoke test twice with a real LLM using an identical user-style prompt: once with the full SKILL.md intact, once with the relevant section removed or corrupted. Assert the first run passes all behavioral assertions and the second run fails — proving the test is sensitive to skill content, not just prompt instructions.

behaviors.description: Raw model output is captured to a diagnostic artifact before any JSON parsing or extraction logic runs.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure this guards against is a pipeline ordering bug: parse/extract logic runs before the raw output is persisted. That ordering only manifests when the capture module and the parsing module are wired together in sequence. A unit test of either module in isolation cannot catch a missing or wrong-order integration wiring. REAL-INFRA: 'Diagnostic artifact' implies a write to the local filesystem, but an in-process fake filesystem (tmp file or StringIO) reproduces the failure. No real network or subprocess is needed. LLM-DEP: The model can be stubbed with a fixed string — the failure is in pipeline ordering, not model behavior. REJECTION-GATE: System would require real OS filesystem events; a local tmpdir suffices.
behaviors.test_description: Inject a fixed raw model output string (bypassing a real LLM call) into the pipeline. Instrument the capture and parsing stages. Assert that the artifact file is written (and contains the unmodified raw string) before the parsing/extraction logic executes — verified by checking file modification timestamps or call order via a spy.

behaviors.description: Assertions verify structured behavior (for example field placement or schema shape), not mere keyword presence that could come from prompt echoing.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: To verify that assertions are structural rather than keyword-based, we need to run the assertion code against crafted model outputs where the two approaches diverge: a response that contains all correct keywords but has wrong structure (should fail structural check, would pass keyword check) and a correct-structure response. This requires the assertion module and output-parsing module to be wired together. A unit test of a single assertion function can verify it rejects one bad case, but the Integration test is needed to confirm the full assertion suite applies structural checks throughout. LLM-DEP: We do not need a real LLM — we craft synthetic outputs that exercise the distinction. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic is not required because crafted string fixtures can precisely target the keyword-vs-structure boundary; real LLM non-determinism is not the failure mode here.
behaviors.test_description: Construct two synthetic model-output strings: one that echoes keywords from the prompt but produces incorrect field placement or schema shape, and one with correct structure. Run the full assertion suite against both. Assert that the keyword-echo output fails at least one assertion and the structurally correct output passes all assertions — confirming assertions are schema/shape-sensitive, not keyword-sensitive.
